refined_fr.refined_fr: Enable measurement and reporting of energy consumption per unit of work output to support evaluation against customer-defined energy efficiency targets.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) the capability to measure/report energy efficiency metrics, and (2) the achievement of a target value. The refined FR is atomic and solution-neutral by focusing on the functional obligation—providing visibility into energy efficiency performance—without prescribing how the system must operate to meet the target. The target itself is a constraint/acceptance criterion, not a functional requirement. This separation allows design freedom in how energy efficiency is optimized (e.g., via drivetrain efficiency, load management, operational scheduling) while ensuring the measurement infrastructure exists.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that the system can capture energy_used (kWh or equivalent) and work_output (tons moved or equivalent task units) over a defined evaluation period, compute energy_per_ton, and report the result in a format accessible to the Site Manager and Project Manager for comparison against E_target. Verify measurement accuracy and completeness across representative operational scenarios.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-003.1: Measure total energy consumed by the NGHE solution during operation.
FR-NGHE-003.2: Measure total work output (e.g., mass moved, distance traveled, or task-specific units) delivered by the NGHE solution during operation.
FR-NGHE-003.3: Calculate and report the energy efficiency metric (energy per unit work output) to authorized stakeholders.

candidate_dps: DP1: Instrument work output (tonnage moved) and energy input (power consumed) independently; aggregate measurements over evaluation period; compute ratio energy_per_ton = total_energy / total_tonnage; compare against E_target threshold.
DP2: Embed real-time energy and work sensors with local data fusion; stream aggregated metrics to centralized reporting system; apply threshold comparison logic at reporting layer.

DP3: Decompose into two independent measurement subsystems: (A) work output measurement via load/displacement sensing, (B) energy consumption measurement via power metering; synchronize timestamps; compute ratio in reporting module.

DP4: Implement continuous monitoring with adaptive sampling: measure energy and work output at variable intervals based on operational state; apply statistical aggregation to handle missing or noisy data; report confidence-bounded efficiency ratio.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP3: Decompose into two independent measurement subsystems: (A) work output measurement via load/displacement sensing, (B) energy consumption measurement via power metering; synchronize timestamps; compute ratio in reporting module.

potential_sources: Timestamp synchronization between work and energy subsystems—mitigated by explicit synchronization protocol at reporting layer rather than tight coupling at sensor level.
Shared data transport infrastructure—acceptable if transport is generic (time-series buffer) rather than domain-specific.
Threshold comparison logic tightly bound to E_target definition—mitigated by parameterizing E_target as external input, not hardcoded.
coupling_check.notes: DP3 achieves low coupling by separating work measurement from energy measurement at the logical level. Each subsystem can evolve independently (e.g., swap load cell for strain gauge without affecting power metering). Synchronization is a thin integration concern, not a functional dependency. DP1 is overly abstract; DP2 conflates sensor fusion with reporting (higher coupling); DP4 introduces statistical complexity not yet justified by requirements.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Work output (tonnage moved) is directly measurable or derivable from load and displacement; risk: if work definition is ambiguous (e.g., partial loads, multi-stage moves), measurement may not reflect true work. Mitigation: clarify work definition in operational policy.

ASSUMPTION: Energy consumption is measurable at a single point (e.g., main power feed); risk: if distributed energy sources exist (e.g., multiple motors, auxiliary systems), single metering may miss consumption. Mitigation: define energy boundary explicitly in acceptance criteria.

ASSUMPTION: Evaluation period is long enough to average transient effects; risk: short periods or highly variable operations may yield noisy efficiency ratios. Mitigation: specify minimum evaluation duration and operational stability criteria.

ASSUMPTION: Timestamp synchronization can be achieved to sufficient precision (e.g., ±1 second); risk: if clock drift or latency is significant, energy-work pairing may be inaccurate. Mitigation: specify synchronization tolerance in DP realization.

ASSUMPTION: E_target is fixed and known at design time; risk: if target is customer-variable or context-dependent, reporting logic must parameterize it. Mitigation: treat E_target as a configuration input, not a design constant.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR should be refined to explicitly state: (1) Definition of 'work output' (e.g., ton·meter, ton moved horizontally, net load lifted); (2) Energy boundary (e.g., main power input, excludes auxiliary systems); (3) Evaluation period duration and operational stability criteria (e.g., minimum 8-hour continuous operation, ≤10% idle time); (4) Acceptable measurement uncertainty (e.g., ±5% on energy_per_ton). These clarifications will reduce ambiguity in DP realization and IM packaging, particularly for sensor selection and data aggregation logic.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Integrated measurement and energy-efficiency computation subsystem encompassing work output sensing, energy consumption metering, timestamp synchronization, and ratio computation for reporting.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Exports synchronized work/energy ratio metrics and raw measurement streams to external monitoring and logging systems.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives measurement configuration directives (sensor calibration, sampling rates, reporting intervals) from task scheduler.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Signals anomalous energy consumption or work output patterns that may indicate system malfunction or safety concern.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Transmits telemetry payloads and receives remote reconfiguration commands for measurement subsystems.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent containment: NGHE Solution is explicitly designed for energy-efficiency measurement and reporting. Both measurement subsystems (work output and energy consumption), timestamp synchronization logic, and ratio computation are core to NGHE's functional scope. Minimal diffusion: only telemetry export and safety alerting leak outward via well-defined interfaces.

Poor containment: Vehicle module is a platform/host, not a measurement-specific subsystem. Placing measurement logic here violates separation of concerns and creates tight coupling between vehicle dynamics and energy analytics. High diffusion: measurement subsystems would scatter across vehicle control layers.

Very poor containment: Teleoperation is a command/control interface layer, not a measurement domain. Embedding work/energy measurement here conflates operator control with system analytics. High diffusion: measurement logic would be entangled with remote command processing.

Weak containment: Safety Services is a reactive alerting layer, not a measurement acquisition and computation layer. While anomaly detection (energy/work ratio) may feed safety decisions, the primary measurement and synchronization logic does not belong here. High diffusion: measurement subsystems would be scattered between Safety Services and other modules.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: DP3 (dual independent measurement subsystems with synchronized timestamps and ratio computation) is a cohesive, domain-specific design pattern that aligns directly with the NGHE Solution's core responsibility: measuring and reporting energy-efficiency metrics. All three sub-components (work output sensing, energy consumption metering, synchronization) are measurement-domain concerns that belong together in a single, well-scoped module. External interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) provide clean boundaries for configuration, reporting, and alerting without requiring measurement logic to diffuse into Vehicle, Teleoperation, or Safety Services.
containment_risks: Risk: If work output sensing requires tight coupling to Vehicle dynamics (e.g., real-time load cell feedback from suspension), the boundary between NGHE Solution and Vehicle may become blurred. Mitigation: Define a clear sensor abstraction layer (IM interface) that Vehicle provides to NGHE Solution, decoupling measurement logic from vehicle control.

Risk: Timestamp synchronization across distributed sensors (load cell, power meter) may require low-latency hardware synchronization or network time protocol (NTP) that spans Vehicle and NGHE Solution. Mitigation: Establish a dedicated time-sync service interface that both modules consume, rather than embedding sync logic in NGHE Solution alone.

Risk: Safety alerting on anomalous energy/work ratios may require real-time feedback loops that Safety Services must own. Mitigation: NGHE Solution computes and exports the ratio; Safety Services consumes it and decides on alerts. Clear producer-consumer boundary.

Risk: Teleoperation may need to adjust measurement parameters (e.g., sampling rate) in response to operator commands. Mitigation: Tasking interface provides the control path; Teleoperation does not directly invoke NGHE Solution.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP3 directly addresses the FR by decomposing energy-efficiency measurement into two independent, synchronizable subsystems (work output via load/displacement sensing; energy consumption via power metering) with explicit ratio computation for reporting. The design is logically sound and traces clearly to the acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target). No logical gaps or contradictions detected.

diffusion_risks: Risk: Work output sensing (load/displacement) may require real-time coupling to Vehicle suspension or drivetrain dynamics. If Vehicle module owns sensor hardware but NGHE Solution owns measurement logic, boundary clarity depends on a well-defined sensor abstraction interface. Mitigation: Establish a Vehicle→NGHE sensor provider interface (e.g., LoadCellReader, DisplacementStream) that decouples measurement acquisition from vehicle control.

Risk: Timestamp synchronization across distributed sensors (load cell, power meter) may require hardware-level clock synchronization or NTP that spans multiple modules. If sync logic is embedded only in NGHE Solution, it may not account for sensor-to-module latency or network jitter. Mitigation: Define a dedicated TimeSync service interface (owned by Vehicle or a shared infrastructure module) that both NGHE Solution and sensors consume, rather than embedding sync logic in NGHE alone.

Risk: SafetyAlert interface signals anomalous energy/work patterns, but Safety Services may need to own the alerting decision logic. If NGHE Solution computes thresholds and triggers alerts directly, ownership becomes ambiguous. Mitigation: NGHE Solution exports the computed ratio and flags; Safety Services consumes and decides on alert severity/action. Clear producer-consumer boundary.

Risk: Telemetry export may require buffering, compression, or filtering of high-frequency measurement streams. If this logic is embedded in NGHE Solution, it couples measurement to communication concerns. Mitigation: NGHE Solution exports raw synchronized streams; a separate Telemetry module handles formatting and transmission.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution achieves 0.92 containment score with 0.08 diffusion. The module is well-scoped for measurement and ratio computation. All three sub-components (work output sensing, energy consumption metering, synchronization) are cohesive measurement-domain concerns. External interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are clean and well-defined. Alternatives (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) show poor containment (0.35–0.15) and high diffusion (0.6–0.85), confirming NGHE Solution as the correct ownership boundary. Key success factor: sensor abstraction layer and time-sync service must be explicitly designed as IM interfaces to prevent boundary blurring with Vehicle and infrastructure modules.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target) is directly computable from NGHE Solution's outputs: synchronized energy consumption (Joules) and work output (ton·m or equivalent). Measurement subsystems are independently testable; ratio computation is deterministic. Telemetry interface enables export of raw and computed metrics for independent validation against customer-defined targets. Worksite safety plan integration is supported via SafetyAlert interface.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. NGHE Solution owns measurement acquisition, synchronization, and ratio computation. Vehicle provides sensor abstraction (load cell, power meter streams). Tasking provides configuration directives. Telemetry consumes and exports results. SafetyAlert consumes anomaly flags. Network Link handles remote reconfiguration. No ambiguity in functional allocation across modules. Ownership boundaries are explicit and non-overlapping.

recommended_revisions: Revision 1: Explicitly define a Sensor Abstraction Layer (IM interface) between Vehicle and NGHE Solution. Specify: (a) LoadCellReader interface (returns force/displacement samples with timestamps), (b) PowerMeterReader interface (returns instantaneous power with timestamps), (c) timestamp resolution and synchronization tolerance (e.g., ±10 ms). This decouples NGHE measurement logic from vehicle hardware details.

Revision 2: Define a TimeSync Service interface (owned by Vehicle or shared infrastructure) that both NGHE Solution and sensors consume. Specify: (a) clock source (e.g., GPS, NTP, hardware PPS), (b) synchronization accuracy (e.g., ±5 ms), (c) latency compensation for sensor-to-module paths. Avoid embedding sync logic in NGHE Solution alone.

Revision 3: Clarify the SafetyAlert interface contract: NGHE Solution exports (energy_per_ton, timestamp, confidence_level); Safety Services consumes and decides on alert thresholds and actions. Document the decision boundary explicitly to prevent ownership ambiguity.

Revision 4: Specify Telemetry export format and buffering strategy. NGHE Solution exports synchronized (work_output, energy_consumption, ratio) tuples at configurable intervals (via Tasking). Telemetry module handles serialization, compression, and transmission. This separates measurement from communication concerns.

Revision 5: Add acceptance test plan: (a) Unit tests for ratio computation (energy/work) with synthetic data, (b) Integration tests for timestamp synchronization across load cell and power meter, (c) End-to-end test validating energy_per_ton ≤ E_target over a representative worksite scenario, (d) Validation that Telemetry exports match NGHE Solution's internal state (no data loss or corruption).

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence. The FR is logically sound, the DP is architecturally viable, and verification is feasible. However, the DP-IM gate identifies five critical diffusion risks that require explicit sub-level decomposition to resolve: (1) sensor abstraction layer boundary clarity, (2) timestamp synchronization service ownership, (3) safety alert decision boundary, (4) telemetry buffering/formatting separation, and (5) acceptance test strategy. These are not design flaws but rather architectural details that must be pushed down to the next level to ensure module interfaces are crisp and ownership is unambiguous. Given high safety criticality, deriving sub-FRs for each of these concerns will enable rigorous interface specification and risk mitigation before implementation.
next_level_sub_frs: FR3.1: Provide a sensor abstraction layer that decouples NGHE measurement logic from Vehicle hardware details, exposing load cell and power meter streams with consistent timestamp semantics.

FR3.2: Synchronize timestamps across distributed sensors (load cell, power meter) to within a specified tolerance (e.g., ±10 ms) using a shared time-sync service, compensating for sensor-to-module latency.
FR3.3: Export energy-per-ton ratio and anomaly flags to Safety Services in a producer-consumer contract that clearly separates measurement computation from alert decision logic.
FR3.4: Buffer, serialize, and transmit synchronized (work_output, energy_consumption, ratio) tuples via Telemetry interface without coupling measurement acquisition to communication concerns.
FR3.5: Enable independent verification of energy_per_ton ≤ E_target through deterministic ratio computation and testable synchronization of measurement subsystems.

revise.why: The DP is sound, but the IM layer requires explicit interface specifications to resolve diffusion risks. Revisions 1–4 from the gate output must be formalized as IM design decisions: (a) Sensor Abstraction Layer interface (LoadCellReader, PowerMeterReader), (b) TimeSync Service interface with latency compensation, (c) SafetyAlert producer-consumer contract, (d) Telemetry export format and buffering strategy. These revisions should be captured as IM sub-components with clear ownership and interface contracts before proceeding to detailed design. Revision 5 (acceptance test plan) should be linked to FR3.5 to ensure verification feasibility is maintained.

assumptions: Sensor hardware (load cell, power meter) is owned and operated by the Vehicle module; NGHE Solution consumes sensor streams via abstraction interfaces.
Timestamp synchronization can be achieved to ±10 ms accuracy using a shared time-sync service (e.g., GPS, NTP, or hardware PPS).
Safety Services owns the decision logic for alert thresholds and actions; NGHE Solution is a data producer only.
Telemetry module is a separate IM responsible for serialization, compression, and transmission; NGHE Solution exports raw synchronized tuples.
Energy-per-ton ratio computation is deterministic and does not require machine learning or adaptive thresholds.
Worksite safety plan integration is achieved via SafetyAlert interface; no additional safety-critical logic is embedded in NGHE Solution.
open_risks: Risk: Sensor abstraction layer may introduce latency or jitter if Vehicle module does not guarantee consistent timestamp resolution. Mitigation: Specify timestamp resolution (e.g., microsecond-level) and latency bounds in LoadCellReader and PowerMeterReader interface contracts.

Risk: TimeSync service may not account for multi-hop network latency if sensors are remote (e.g., wireless load cells). Mitigation: Define latency compensation strategy in TimeSync interface; validate with representative sensor deployment topology.

Risk: SafetyAlert interface may be misused if Safety Services does not consume NGHE outputs correctly (e.g., ignoring confidence_level flag). Mitigation: Establish formal producer-consumer contract with explicit error handling and fallback behavior.

Risk: Telemetry buffering may drop high-frequency samples if buffer size is insufficient or transmission bandwidth is limited. Mitigation: Specify buffer size, overflow policy (drop oldest vs. drop newest), and sampling rate in Telemetry interface contract.

Risk: Acceptance test plan may not cover edge cases (e.g., sensor failure, clock skew, network partition). Mitigation: Extend test plan to include fault injection and resilience scenarios; validate against safety-critical requirements.
evidence_needed: Sensor Abstraction Layer specification: LoadCellReader and PowerMeterReader interface contracts with timestamp semantics, latency bounds, and error handling.
TimeSync Service specification: clock source, synchronization accuracy, latency compensation algorithm, and validation against representative sensor topologies.
SafetyAlert producer-consumer contract: NGHE Solution output schema (energy_per_ton, timestamp, confidence_level), Safety Services decision logic, and fallback behavior on interface failure.
Telemetry export format specification: serialization format (JSON, protobuf, etc.), buffering strategy, sampling rate, and transmission protocol.

Acceptance test plan: unit tests for ratio computation, integration tests for timestamp synchronization, end-to-end tests for energy_per_ton ≤ E_target, and fault injection tests for sensor failure and network partition scenarios.
Validation of timestamp synchronization accuracy across load cell and power meter in representative worksite environment (e.g., field trial with GPS-disciplined oscillator as reference).
Safety case documentation linking NGHE Solution outputs to worksite safety plan and demonstrating that SafetyAlert interface enables timely anomaly detection.
